blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: The thread ceremony of this tree is strongly recommended along with its marriage with Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum). Its dry twigs are used in the worship of the sacred fire (to feed it).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Halad. Powdered tubers of the Curcuma longa, turmeric, form an important item of the worship of all gods. It is also used as a cosmetic by women whose husbands are alive. No widow is allowed to use it. Mixed with slaked lime its powder (which becomes red) is applied by married women to the forehead.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Jack fruit tree. Artocarpus integrifolia. The Nambudri Brahmans of Malabar produce sacred fire by friction of the dry branches of this tree.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Karle. Momordia dioica. It is considered inauspicious to pass under this creeper and it is supposed to have some influence over the lives of children. Mothers and women desirous of children never pass under its shadow (p. 50).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Khas or khus (p. 102). Andropogon muricata, Syn., Vete veria odorata. Its fragrant roots known as Khas are much in use. (See Khas Shasthi for folklore.)

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Kush grass. Eragrotis cynosuroides, is held sacred and considered necessary as a mat to the pitris. The eldest son of Rāma was named Kush after this grass. In the Chaturmās Mahātmya this grass is said to be the transformation of Ketu. The Shrāvan purān directs that on the Pithori day (which please see) this grass should be pulled out if required for ceremonial purposes, such as funerals and shrādhas.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Kushmand. Cucurbita maxima or pepo. This fruit represents the buffalo-demon sacrificed on the Dasara day. It is also considered a great merit to hollow this fruit, fill it up with gold and jewels and to present it to a Brahman as
